topic: A person's manuscript is rejected by their dream publisher
raw_output:
[story 1]

The thick, cream-colored envelope felt heavy in Clara’s hands, the embossed seal of 'Aethelred & Sons' mocking her with its polished perfection. She reread the single, curt line in the rejection letter, the ink seeming to burn the paper. *“We have decided not to move forward with your submission.”* Clara slowly lowered the letter onto the mahogany desk, the sound echoing too loudly in the quiet study. Her jaw tightened, a hard, inflexible line appearing beneath her skin. She picked up her pen, but instead of writing, she began methodically tearing the pages out of the manuscript, the crisp sound of the tearing paper a sharp, aggressive counterpoint to the silence. She stood up abruptly, pacing the length of the room, her heels clicking a frantic, uneven rhythm against the Persian rug. Every nerve ending felt taut, vibrating with a cold, simmering resentment that made her palms sweat. She needed to smash something, anything, to release the pressure building behind her ribs.

[story 2]

Mark stared at the screen, the rejection email glowing harshly against the dark background of his laptop. It was from Zenith Press, the publisher he’d poured two years of his soul into cultivating a relationship with. He reread the polite, sterile dismissal, the words washing over him like a physical slap. A low growl escaped his throat, a sound rusty from disuse. He slammed his fist onto the desk, rattling the ceramic mug beside it. The liquid sloshed over the rim, staining the wood. He didn't move for a full minute, just breathing in short, sharp gasps, trying to force the heat churning in his chest down. When he finally looked up, his eyes were blazing, fixed on the closed door of his apartment. He began aggressively shuffling through his notes, ripping out passages he thought were perfect, crumpling them into tight, angry balls, and tossing them into the wastebasket with unnecessary force.

[story 3]

Eleanor received the notification while waiting in line at the convention center. The sheer audacity of the rejection—from the very house she’d aimed for—made her stomach clench into a tight knot. She kept her hands clasped so tightly together that her knuckles turned white. When the attendant handed her the thin packet, Eleanor didn't look at it immediately. Instead, she took a deep, shuddering breath, the air catching painfully in her throat. Her lips pressed into a thin, white line. She forced herself to look at the letter, her gaze lingering on the formal signatory line as if searching for some hidden insult. Then, with a sudden, sharp movement, she shoved the packet deep into her oversized tote bag, the movement jerky and uncontrolled. She walked away from the counter quickly, her steps purposeful and hurried, the tension radiating off her body like heat from a furnace.
